title: European Literature
intro: European Literature questions draw heavily on a handful of dense national traditions rather than spreading evenly across the continent. Russian literature is the single largest cluster, anchored by Tolstoy, Dostoevsky, Gogol, Chekhov, and Pushkin in the nineteenth century and extended into the twentieth by Bulgakov, Nabokov, Pasternak, and Solzhenitsyn; German-language literature forms a comparably deep vein running from Goethe and Schiller through Kafka, Mann, Rilke, Hesse, and Brecht. French literature is asked about across the widest span of centuries, from Moliere, Racine, and Voltaire through the nineteenth-century novel of Balzac, Flaubert, and Zola into Symbolist poetry and the Absurdist theater of Ionesco, Beckett, and Genet.

Italian and Iberian letters supply a second tier of frequently asked material: Dante, Petrarch, and Boccaccio as the founders of vernacular literature, the Spanish Golden Age of Cervantes, Lope de Vega, and Calderon, and a strong twentieth-century Italian postmodern strand led by Calvino, Eco, and Pirandello. Scandinavian drama, dominated by Ibsen and Strindberg, and a cluster of Central and Eastern European writers shaped by war, occupation, and Soviet rule (Kafka's Czech contemporaries, Milosz, Kundera, Kadare, and the modern Greek poets Cavafy and Kazantzakis) round out the canon's other major regions.

Classical antiquity supplies a steady undercurrent throughout the unit, both as source material referenced by later authors and as its own answerline space of Greek tragedians, comic playwrights, and Roman poets. A significant share of questions also resolve to answerlines that are not authors or titles at all, but recurring words, motifs, and historical backdrops (war, exile, disease, dreams, common objects) that quizbowl writers use as common links across many national traditions at once, or to English and Irish authors invoked for comparison alongside their continental peers.

sections.name: Classical Antiquity
sections.blurb: Greek and Roman poets, dramatists, and epics that form the bedrock of the Western literary tradition.

entries.note: Sophocles's tragedy of a heroine who defies Creon's edict to bury her brother Polynices.
Greek lyric poet of Lesbos famed for intense personal odes to female beloveds.
Mythic musician who descended to the underworld for Eurydice; a common link across many operas and poems.

Mythic daughter of Agamemnon who helps avenge his murder, dramatized across Greek tragedy.

sections.name: Medieval Literature
sections.blurb: Epics, romances, and courtly verse from pre-1400 Europe outside Italy, including chansons de geste and troubadour lyric.

entries.note: Hero of the Song of Roland, Charlemagne's knight betrayed by Ganelon at Roncevaux.
French medieval poet-vagabond; wrote "The Testament," blending ballad forms with themes of mortality.
Occitan courtly-love poets of medieval southern France.
Frankish emperor whose court appears in the Song of Roland and Carolingian-cycle epic literature.
Medieval French poet of the Lais, short romantic verse narratives.
Medieval French court writer; wrote The Book of the City of Ladies defending women.
Spanish medieval epic hero, based on knight Rodrigo Diaz, also dramatized by Corneille.
Language of the medieval troubadours of southern France.
French epic (chanson de geste) of Charlemagne's knight Roland's last stand at Roncevaux, betrayed by Ganelon.
Treacherous knight who betrays Roland to the Saracens in the Song of Roland.
Allegorical medieval French poem of a lover's quest to win a rose symbolizing his beloved.
Sacred vessel sought by knights in Arthurian romance, notably Wolfram von Eschenbach's Parzival.
Central figures of chivalric romance across medieval European epic and legend.
Crusading military order featured in medieval and later historical fiction.
Wolfram von Eschenbach's Arthurian romance of a knight's quest for the Holy Grail.

sections.name: Italian Literature
sections.blurb: From Dante and Boccaccio's founding of vernacular Italian letters through Pirandello, Calvino, and Eco's postmodern experiments.

entries.note: Italian postmodernist and Oulipo member; wrote Invisible Cities, If on a Winter's Night a Traveler, and Cosmicomics.
Italian semiotician-novelist; wrote The Name of the Rose and Foucault's Pendulum, blending medieval mystery with theory.
Italian modernist of the Theatre of the Grotesque; wrote Six Characters in Search of an Author.
Italian humanist whose Canzoniere sonnets to Laura shaped the Renaissance lyric tradition.
Home of Dante, Petrarch, and Boccaccio, and modern postmodernists like Calvino and Eco.
Boccaccio's frame narrative of ten storytellers exchanging tales while fleeing the Black Death in Florence.
Language linking Dante, Petrarch, and Boccaccio to modern authors like Calvino and Eco.
Eco's medieval murder mystery set in a monastery, narrated by novice Adso alongside William of Baskerville.
Italian humanist; wrote the frame-narrative Decameron, foundational to European prose fiction.

entries.topic: If on a Winter's Night a Traveler
entries.answerline: If on a Winter's Night a Traveler

Pseudonymous Italian novelist; wrote the Neapolitan Quartet, including My Brilliant Friend.
Italian-Jewish chemist and Holocaust memoirist; wrote If This Is a Man and The Periodic Table.

Second canticle of Dante's Divine Comedy, ascending the mountain of Purgatory.

Eco's picaresque novel of a compulsive liar recounting his adventures around the Fourth Crusade.
Dario Fo's farce satirizing a police cover-up of a suspicious death in custody.

sections.name: French Literature
sections.blurb: France's continuous literary dominance from Classicism and the Enlightenment through Romanticism, Realism, Symbolism, and the Theatre of the Absurd.

French Naturalist master of the short story; wrote "Boule de Suif" and "The Necklace."
French modernist author of In Search of Lost Time, exploring involuntary memory via the madeleine episode.
French Classical comic playwright; wrote Tartuffe, The Misanthrope, and The Imaginary Invalid.
French Realist perfectionist; wrote Madame Bovary and Sentimental Education.

Sartre's existentialist reworking of the Orestes myth, exploring freedom and guilt.
Saint-Exupery's fable of a young prince visiting Earth from a tiny asteroid.
Genet's Absurdist play of two servants ritually enacting murderous fantasies against their mistress.
Beauvoir's novel following French intellectuals navigating politics after World War II.
Dumas's swashbuckling novel of d'Artagnan joining Athos, Porthos, and Aramis.
Balzac's fantastical novel of a magic skin that grants wishes while shortening its owner's life.
Mid-20th-century dramatic movement of illogical, existentially bleak comedy led by Ionesco and Beckett.
Full name of the French Romantic author of Les Miserables.

entries.topic: "A Throw of the Dice Will Never Abolish Chance"
entries.answerline: "A Throw of the Dice Will Never Abolish Chance"
entries.note: Mallarme's Symbolist poem experimenting with typography and blank space across the page.
Rimbaud's Symbolist poem narrated by a runaway, sinking boat's hallucinatory voyage.
French Nobel laureate memoirist known for spare autobiographical prose.
Racine's biblical tragedy of a usurping queen's downfall in ancient Judah.
Voltaire's tutor in Candide who insists all is for the best despite endless catastrophe.
Full name of the playwright who wrote Cyrano de Bergerac.
Beaumarchais's clever barber-valet hero of The Barber of Seville and The Marriage of Figaro.
Central image of Baudelaire's Les Fleurs du Mal (The Flowers of Evil).
French Romantic poet of dreamlike prose; wrote "El Desdichado" and Aurelia.
Lesage's picaresque novel following a young Spaniard's rise through varied fortunes.
Full name of the French Neoclassical tragedian who wrote Phedre.
Full name of the French Symbolist poet of A Season in Hell.
Stendhal's ambitious, hypocritical protagonist in The Red and the Black.
Marquis de Sade's novel of a virtuous woman's relentless suffering and abuse.
Zola's Naturalist novel of a laundress's descent into poverty and alcoholism.
Zola's Naturalist novel of hereditary violence centered on a railway worker.
Balzac's novel of a devoted father ruined by his daughters' ingratitude, alongside the schemer Vautrin.
French (Belgian-born) novelist; wrote Memoirs of Hadrian.
Cunning femme fatale antagonist in Dumas's The Three Musketeers.
Zola's Naturalist novel of a courtesan's rise and destructive effect on Parisian society.
Hugo's novel (The Hunchback of Notre-Dame) of the bellringer Quasimodo's love for Esmeralda.
French Symbolist-adjacent poet; wrote "The Graveyard by the Sea."
Balzac's novel of a devoted father ruined by his daughters' ingratitude.
French dramatist; wrote The Barber of Seville and The Marriage of Figaro.
French Renaissance poet and leader of the Pleiade group.
French Oulipo co-founder; wrote Exercises in Style and Zazie in the Metro.
French literary theorist and semiotician; wrote S/Z and Mythologies.
Recurring mood of ennui and disgust in Baudelaire's Les Fleurs du Mal.
Ionesco's Absurdist play of a tutoring session that escalates into the professor's murder of his student.
Mid-20th-century dramatic movement of illogical, existentially bleak comedy led by Ionesco and Beckett.
Mauriac's protagonist in Therese Desqueyroux, who poisons her husband.
Subject of Rimbaud's sonnet assigning colors to each vowel.
Flaubert's story of a servant woman's quiet, devoted life and simple faith.
Baudelaire's poem imagining an idealized escape with his beloved to a land of "luxury, calm, and pleasure."
Zola's open letter defending Alfred Dreyfus against his antisemitic false conviction.
Maupassant's story of a man tormented by an invisible, possibly supernatural presence.
Maupassant's story of a prostitute who kills a boastful Prussian officer.
Maupassant's story of a man tormented by an invisible, possibly supernatural presence.
Maupassant's story of a peasant wrongly accused of theft over a picked-up scrap of string.
Baudelaire's poem meditating on exile and loss, inspired by a swan in a Paris under renovation.
Balzac's story of a painter whose obsessive perfectionism ruins his final canvas.
Huysmans's Decadent novel of an aesthete's reclusive pursuit of artificial, refined sensation.
Perec's Oulipo novel written entirely without the letter "e."
French novelist-dramatist son of Dumas pere; wrote The Lady of the Camellias.
Twelve-syllable poetic line standard in French Classical drama, including Racine's tragedies.
Plural form of the twelve-syllable line standard in French Classical tragedy.
French Romantic poet-dramatist; wrote the autobiographical novel The Confession of a Child of the Century.
French Jewish military officer wrongly convicted of treason, defended by Zola's "J'Accuse."
French proto-Absurdist; wrote Ubu Roi and founded the "science" of Pataphysics.
French novelist-statesman; wrote Man's Fate, set during the Shanghai uprising.
Full name of the French modernist Nobel laureate author of The Immoralist.
Title of Villiers de l'Isle-Adam's Symbolist drama, source of the line "As for living, our servants will do that for us."
Sagan's novel of a teenage girl's manipulative sabotage of her father's romance.
Full French title of Voltaire's satire mocking Leibnizian optimism.
French author who codified fairy tales like "Cinderella" and "Little Red Riding Hood."
Island prison where Dumas's Edmond Dantes is unjustly held in The Count of Monte Cristo.
Voltaire's beautiful love interest whom Candide pursues across catastrophes.
Family name of the Romantic novelists (pere and fils) behind The Three Musketeers and The Lady of the Camellias.
Full name of the French Naturalist author of Germinal and the Rougon-Macquart cycle.
Balzac's ambitious young protagonist recurring across the Human Comedy, notably in Pere Goriot.
French Romantic writer; wrote Rene and the memoir Memoirs from Beyond the Grave.
French novelist; wrote Bonjour Tristesse as a teenager.
1789 upheaval forming the backdrop of Buchner's Danton's Death and countless French novels.
Rabelais's satirical Renaissance epic of two grotesque, appetite-driven giants.
French farceur known for fast-paced comedies of mistaken identity and adultery.
Hugo's Romantic drama whose riotous premiere marked a turning point against Classical rules.
French avant-garde writer-filmmaker; wrote Les Enfants Terribles and reworked the Orpheus myth on film.
Full name of the French pioneer of science fiction.
French precieuse novelist known for lengthy romances like Artamene.
Aristocratic love interest whose passion helps ruin Julien Sorel in Stendhal's The Red and the Black.
Yourcenar's novel imagining the Roman emperor's reflective final testament.
French Renaissance essayist who originated the personal essay form.
French nouveau roman novelist known for fragmented psychological "tropisms."
French Surrealist poet; wrote Capital of Pain.
19th-century movement of faithfully depicting everyday life, codified by Balzac, Stendhal, and Flaubert.
Late-19th-century movement of suggestive, musical verse led by Baudelaire, Mallarme, and Verlaine.
Destination of Jules Verne's adventurers in Journey to the Center of the Earth.
Robbe-Grillet's nouveau roman mystery subverting detective-novel conventions.
Gide's novel of a man's self-liberation into hedonism after recovering from illness.
Duras's autobiographical novel of a teenage girl's affair with an older man in colonial Vietnam.
Moliere's comedy of a man whose brutal honesty alienates polite society.
Lafayette's novel, often called the first French psychological novel, of a married woman's forbidden love.
Flaubert's experimental drama of the hermit saint's hallucinatory trials.
Central theme of Jules Verne's Voyages Extraordinaires adventure novels.
French playwright; wrote the comedy Art, on a controversial all-white painting.

sections.name: German-Language Literature
sections.blurb: Writers of Germany, Austria, and Switzerland spanning Sturm und Drang through Weimar Classicism, Romanticism, and Modernism.

entries.note: German polymath of Weimar Classicism; wrote Faust and The Sorrows of Young Werther, launching Sturm und Drang.
Prague-born German-language modernist; wrote The Trial, The Castle, and The Metamorphosis on alienation and bureaucracy.
Austrian modernist poet; wrote Duino Elegies and Sonnets to Orpheus on beauty, mortality, and transcendence.
German Marxist dramatist who developed Epic Theatre's alienation effect; wrote Mother Courage and The Caucasian Chalk Circle.
German modernist novelist; wrote Buddenbrooks, Death in Venice, and The Magic Mountain.
Language of Goethe, Kafka, Mann, and Rilke; a recurring common-link descriptor across the unit.
German Sturm und Drang and Weimar Classicism dramatist; wrote The Robbers and William Tell.
German-Swiss Nobel laureate; wrote Siddhartha, Steppenwolf, and The Glass Bead Game.
German Romantic poet; wrote "The Lorelei" and satirical political verse.
Mann's novella of aging writer Gustav von Aschenbach's obsession with a boy amid a cholera outbreak.
Rilke's ten elegies, begun at Duino Castle, on beauty, mortality, and confronting angels.
German Romantic; wrote the novella Michael Kohlhaas and the comedy The Broken Jug.
Home of German-language writers Hermann Hesse and Max Frisch.
Home of Rilke, Kafka's Habsburg-era milieu, and fin-de-siecle Vienna's literary culture.
German Romantic writer of fantastical tales; wrote "The Sandman" and Tomcat Murr, inspiring The Nutcracker ballet.
Goethe's tragedy of a scholar who bargains his soul to Mephistopheles for knowledge and pleasure.
German Group 47 novelist of postwar Trummerliteratur; wrote Billiards at Half-Past Nine.
Hesse's utopian intellectual game at the center of his final novel, Das Glasperlenspiel.
German early Romantic poet associated with the symbol of the blue flower.
German Nobel laureate; wrote The Tin Drum, narrated by Oskar Matzerath.
Central figures of terrifying beauty in Rilke's Duino Elegies.
Schiller's Sturm und Drang drama of rebellious brothers Karl and Franz Moor.

entries.topic: All Quiet on the Western Front
entries.answerline: All Quiet on the Western Front
entries.note: Remarque's World War I novel narrated by soldier Paul Baumer.
German Romantic-era poet of hymn-like odes; wrote Hyperion; later succumbed to mental illness.
German Naturalist Nobel laureate playwright; wrote The Weavers.
Full name of the German Group 47 Nobel laureate novelist.
Movement of emotion and nature over reason, spanning Goethe, Novalis, and Hoffmann in German lands.
Mann's novel set at a Swiss tuberculosis sanatorium, following patient Hans Castorp.
Grass's novel narrated by Oskar Matzerath, who refuses to grow past age three.
Kafka's novel of Josef K., arrested and prosecuted by an inaccessible, absurd court.
Swiss dramatist; wrote The Visit and The Physicists.
Home of Goethe, Schiller, Kafka's German-language milieu, Mann, and Rilke.
Late-18th-century German movement of emotional intensity led by young Goethe and Schiller.
Kafka's story of a torturous execution device that inscribes the condemned's sentence on their body.
German dramatist who died young; wrote Danton's Death and the unfinished Woyzeck.
Hesse's novel following its title character's spiritual quest alongside the Buddha's era.
Musil's unfinished modernist novel satirizing Austrian society before World War I.
Kafka's novella of traveling salesman Gregor Samsa waking transformed into an insect.
Goethe's epistolary novel of unrequited love ending in suicide, sparking a Romantic craze.
Rilke's sonnet on a headless statue whose gaze demands "you must change your life."
Hoffmann's uncanny tale of Nathanael's obsession with the automaton Olympia.
German novelist of World War I; wrote All Quiet on the Western Front.
Austrian postwar poet and novelist associated with Group 47.
Rilke's epistolary advice to an aspiring poet on solitude and creative discipline.
Brecht's Epic Theatre protagonist, a canteen woman profiting from the Thirty Years' War.
Brecht's Epic Theatre play in which a canteen woman loses all her children to war.
Grass's narrator in The Tin Drum who wills himself to stop growing.
Romanian-born Jewish German-language poet; wrote "Death Fugue" on the Holocaust.
Austrian modernist; wrote the unfinished epic The Man Without Qualities.
Wedekind's play on adolescent sexual awakening and repressive education.
Austrian novelist-essayist; wrote Chess Story and the memoir The World of Yesterday.
Hesse's novel of the self-divided "wolf of the steppes," Harry Haller.
Kafka's unfinished novel of K.'s futile attempts to gain access to an inscrutable castle bureaucracy.
Brecht's Epic Theatre parable of a servant girl's disputed custody of a child, judged via a chalk circle.
Celan's Holocaust poem repeating the haunting image of "black milk of daybreak."
Kafka's unfinished novel following young immigrant Karl Rossmann in a surreal United States.
Mann's novel chronicling a merchant family's generational decline.
Anti-art movement founded in Zurich by Hugo Ball and Tristan Tzara during World War I.
German title of Hesse's novel of the self-divided Harry Haller.
Fontane's Realist novel of a young wife's adultery and its tragic social consequences.
Austrian Nobel laureate novelist; wrote The Piano Teacher.
German Enlightenment dramatist-critic; wrote Nathan the Wise and Emilia Galotti.
Full name of the German Nobel laureate author of The Tin Drum.
Mann's protagonist in The Magic Mountain, a young engineer detained at a Swiss sanatorium.
Variant spelling of the German-Swiss Nobel laureate author of Siddhartha and Steppenwolf.
Home of Rilke, Kafka's Habsburg milieu, Musil, and Zweig.
Swiss modernist writer of understated, wandering prose; wrote The Walk.
Rilke's cycle of fifty-five sonnets meditating on transformation through the myth of Orpheus.
Protagonist of Goethe's Bildungsroman Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship.
Coming-of-age novel genre exemplified by Goethe's Wilhelm Meister and Hesse's Demian.
Buchner's play dramatizing the French Revolution's Reign of Terror and Danton's fall.
Mann's novel of composer Adrian Leverkuhn's demonic pact, allegorizing Germany's fall into Nazism.
Lessing's bourgeois tragedy of a virtuous woman destroyed by a prince's desire.
Language of Goethe, Kafka, Mann, and Rilke.
Kafka's traveling-salesman protagonist who wakes transformed into a giant insect in The Metamorphosis.
Mann's aging writer whose obsession with a beautiful boy leads to his death in Death in Venice.
Romanian-German Nobel laureate; writes on totalitarian repression under Ceausescu.
German co-founder of Dada in Zurich, known for sound poetry.
Hesse's novel contrasting a contemplative monk and a worldly wanderer's friendship.
Austrian Nobel laureate playwright-novelist; wrote Offending the Audience.
Setting of Mann's The Magic Mountain, where tuberculosis patients convalesce in the Alps.
Ballet based on Hoffmann's tale "The Nutcracker and the Mouse King."
Durrenmatt's play of a wealthy woman who returns to her hometown offering money for a former lover's death.
Devastating 17th-century religious conflict forming the backdrop of Grimmelshausen's Simplicius Simplicissimus.
Austrian novelist-playwright known for scathing, repetitive prose; wrote The Loser.
Disease afflicting patients at the Swiss sanatorium in Mann's The Magic Mountain.
German writer of hybrid memoir-fiction; wrote Austerlitz, blending photography and prose.
Goethe's lovesick protagonist whose suicide over Lotte sparked a Romantic fashion trend.
Heine's poem of a siren luring Rhine sailors to their deaths with her song.
Boll's novel tracing three generations of a German family across two world wars.
Grass's novella on the sinking of the Wilhelm Gustloff and postwar German memory.
Anti-art movement founded in wartime Zurich, rejecting logic and tradition.
Hesse's Bildungsroman of young Emil Sinclair's spiritual awakening guided by the enigmatic Max Demian.
German writer of the World War I memoir Storm of Steel.
German philosopher of the "will to power" and eternal recurrence; wrote Thus Spoke Zarathustra.
Early-19th-century movement of Novalis, Hoffmann, and Kleist emphasizing emotion and folklore.
German novelist addressing 20th-century displacement and division; wrote Go, Went, Gone.
Full name of the German Romantic-era poet of Hyperion.
Sorcerer antagonist in Wolfram von Eschenbach's Parzival, also featured in Wagner's opera.
Peter Weiss's play staging the Marquis de Sade directing inmates in a drama about Marat's assassination.
Lessing's Enlightenment drama arguing for religious tolerance among Judaism, Christianity, and Islam.
German playwright; wrote Marat/Sade, staging revolutionary history within an asylum.
Bernhard's novel of a pianist's obsessive rivalry with genius Glenn Gould.
Romanian-born co-founder of Dada in Zurich.
Austrian capital and fin-de-siecle cultural center of Rilke, Freud, and Zweig.
Kafka's story narrated by an ape who has learned to imitate humans to escape captivity.
Kafka's story narrated by a paranoid burrowing creature obsessed with securing its tunnel.
Kafka's story of a son condemned to drown himself by his dying father's curse.
Rilke's poem depicting a caged panther's numbed, pacing despair.
Closing line of Rilke's sonnet "Archaic Torso of Apollo."
Thirty Years' War general dramatized in Schiller's Wallenstein trilogy.
Sebald's novel following a man's fragmented search for his lost childhood identity amid the Holocaust.
German capital and setting for much modern German fiction.
Symbol of longing and the infinite in Novalis's unfinished novel Heinrich von Ofterdingen.

entries.topic: Buddenbrooks: The Decline of a Family
entries.answerline: Buddenbrooks: The Decline of a Family
entries.note: Full title of Mann's novel chronicling a merchant family's generational decline.
Goethe's married love interest in The Sorrows of Young Werther.
Baltic port city (Gdansk) and setting of Grass's The Tin Drum.
Mann's novel of composer Adrian Leverkuhn's demonic pact, allegorizing Germany's fall into Nazism.
Double or ghostly duplicate figure, a recurring device in Hoffmann's and Dostoevsky's fiction.
Goethe's novel using chemical bonding as a metaphor for marital infidelity.
Mann's charming con-man protagonist in Confessions of Felix Krull, Confidence Man.
German Enlightenment dramatist-critic; wrote Nathan the Wise and Emilia Galotti.
Goethe's tragic love interest in Faust, seduced and driven to infanticide and madness.
Postwar German writers' collective including Boll and Grass, shaping Trummerliteratur.
Common German first name, notably novelist Gunter Grass.
Common German first name shared by Heine, Boll, and Mann's brother Heinrich.
Goethe's epic poem of a refugee romance set against the French Revolutionary Wars.
Austrian poet-librettist who collaborated with composer Richard Strauss.
Holderlin's epistolary novel of an idealistic Greek revolutionary's disillusionment.
Form Gregor Samsa awakens transformed into in Kafka's The Metamorphosis.
Schiller's Sturm und Drang tragedy of star-crossed lovers across a class divide.
Pen name of German Romantic novelist Johann Paul Friedrich Richter.
Full name of the German Sturm und Drang and Weimar Classicism dramatist.
Master of the intellectual glass-bead game in Hesse's final novel.
Austrian satirist-journalist; wrote the vast anti-war drama The Last Days of Mankind.
Buchner's novella depicting poet Jakob Lenz's descent into mental illness.
Recurring pastoral image in German Romantic poetry.
Swiss novelist-dramatist; wrote Homo Faber and the play Andorra.
The devil who bargains for Faust's soul in Goethe's tragedy.
Kleist's novella of a horse dealer whose quest for justice spirals into rebellion.
German-Jewish Nobel laureate poet of Holocaust mourning and exile.
Remarque's young soldier protagonist narrating All Quiet on the Western Front.
Postwar German genre (Trummerliteratur) confronting the physical and moral wreckage of World War II.
Setting of Mann's The Magic Mountain, where tuberculosis patients convalesce.
Grimmelshausen's picaresque novel of a naif's survival through the Thirty Years' War.
Rilke's early poetry collection of devotional, mystical verse.
Kleist's comedy of a corrupt judge investigating a crime he himself committed.
Boll's novel of a disillusioned entertainer estranged from postwar German Catholic society.
Setting of Kafka's story depicting a torturous execution device.
Durrenmatt's play of scientists feigning madness in an asylum to avoid weaponizing their discoveries.
Hauptmann's Naturalist play dramatizing a Silesian weavers' uprising.
Hoffmann's novel narrated partly by a self-important, philosophizing cat.
Mann's novella of an artist torn between bourgeois normalcy and creative alienation.
Central ordeal of Kafka's The Trial, in which Josef K. is prosecuted by an inaccessible court.
German-Jewish critic-philosopher; wrote "The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction."
Goethe's lovesick protagonist whose suicide over Lotte sparked a Romantic fashion trend.
Schiller's drama dramatizing the legendary Swiss archer's revolt against Habsburg tyranny.
Full name of the German writer of the hybrid memoir-fiction novel Austerlitz.
Central image in Hesse's Steppenwolf, the "wolf of the steppes."

sections.name: Russian Literature
sections.blurb: The nineteenth-century golden age of Pushkin, Gogol, Tolstoy, and Dostoevsky through Soviet-era and dissident writing.

entries.note: Russian master of understated realist drama and short story; wrote The Cherry Orchard, Three Sisters, and Uncle Vanya.
Founding poet of modern Russian literature; wrote Eugene Onegin and "The Bronze Horseman."
Acmeist Silver Age poet; wrote Requiem, mourning victims of Stalinist repression.
Ukrainian-Russian writer of Dead Souls and "The Overcoat," blending grotesque satire with Romanticism.
Russian Realist; wrote War and Peace and Anna Karenina, later embracing Christian anarchism.
Russian-American emigre novelist; wrote Lolita and Pale Fire, bridging Russian and English traditions.
Russian Realist; wrote Fathers and Sons, dramatizing the generational nihilist conflict.
Russian founder of Socialist Realism; wrote the play The Lower Depths.
Soviet satirist; wrote The Master and Margarita, in which the Devil upends Stalinist Moscow.
Bulgakov's satire in which Satan and his retinue upend Stalinist Moscow alongside Pontius Pilate chapters.
Russian existentialist-realist; wrote Crime and Punishment, The Brothers Karamazov, and The Idiot.
Language of Pushkin, Tolstoy, Dostoevsky, and Chekhov.
Dostoevsky's final novel of three brothers and a patricide, weighing faith against rationalism via the Grand Inquisitor.
Russian Nobel laureate forced to decline the prize; wrote Doctor Zhivago, spanning the Russian Revolution.
Gogol's satirical novel of con man Chichikov buying dead serfs' names to inflate his estate.
Chekhov's drama of wasted rural lives and quiet despair on a country estate.
Soviet dissident Nobel laureate; wrote One Day in the Life of Ivan Denisovich and The Gulag Archipelago.
Pasternak's novel of physician-poet Yuri Zhivago's love amid the Russian Revolution and Civil War.
Turgenev's novel dramatizing the nihilist Bazarov's clash with the older generation.
Tolstoy's novel of an adulterous affair ending in the heroine's suicide beneath a train.
Pushkin's verse novel of a bored aristocrat who kills his friend Lensky in a duel.
Chekhov's drama of thwarted artistic ambition and unrequited love at a country estate.
Chekhov's story of an adulterous affair begun at a Yalta seaside resort.
Full name of the founding poet of modern Russian literature; wrote Eugene Onegin.

entries.topic: One Day in the Life of Ivan Denisovich
entries.answerline: One Day in the Life of Ivan Denisovich
entries.note: Solzhenitsyn's novel depicting a single day in a Soviet gulag labor camp.
Nabokov's novel structured as a poem with an unreliable, delusional annotator's commentary.
Gogol's story of a poor clerk's obsession with a new coat, said to have spawned Russian realism.
Nabokov's novel narrated by Humbert Humbert, obsessed with the young Dolores Haze.
Russian Romantic poet-novelist; wrote A Hero of Our Time, featuring the "superfluous man" Pechorin.
Home of Pushkin, Tolstoy, Dostoevsky, and Chekhov.
Russian imperial capital setting Gogol's, Dostoevsky's, and Pushkin's "The Bronze Horseman."
Dostoevsky's novel of the saintly, epileptic Prince Myshkin navigating corrupt St. Petersburg society.
Chekhov's drama of an aristocratic family forced to sell their beloved estate.
Dostoevsky's novel of student Raskolnikov's murder of a pawnbroker and ensuing guilt.
Full name of the author of Dead Souls and "The Overcoat."
Communist state whose censorship and gulags shaped Bulgakov, Solzhenitsyn, and Pasternak.
Gorky's play depicting impoverished lodgers in a squalid Russian flophouse.
Lermontov's novel introducing the disaffected "superfluous man" Pechorin.
Bulgakov's satirical novella of a dog surgically transformed into a boorish Soviet man.
Pushkin's narrative poem of a clerk driven mad by Peter the Great's equestrian statue during a flood.
Alternate spelling of the Russian existentialist-realist author of Crime and Punishment.
Full name of the author of The Brothers Karamazov.
Anna Karenina's betrayed government-official husband in Tolstoy's novel.
Griboyedov's verse comedy satirizing Moscow high society through the disillusioned Chatsky.

entries.topic: "How Much Land Does a Man Need?"
entries.answerline: "How Much Land Does a Man Need?"
entries.note: Tolstoy's parable of a peasant's fatal greed for ever more land.
Full name of the Russian master of realist drama and short story.
Soviet forced-labor camp system documented by Solzhenitsyn.
Full name of the Russian Realist author of Fathers and Sons.
Russian literary theorist known for concepts of dialogism and the carnivalesque.
Goncharov's novel of an aristocrat so lethargic he cannot leave his bed.
Roman prefect who appears in the parallel biblical narrative of Bulgakov's The Master and Margarita.
Akhmatova's cycle mourning victims of Stalin's purges, written while her son was imprisoned.
Official Soviet artistic doctrine championed by Maxim Gorky.
Tolstoy's novella confronting a dying bureaucrat's realization that his life was wasted.
Recurring tempter figure, notably Woland in Bulgakov's The Master and Margarita.
Russian Futurist poet who championed the Bolshevik Revolution before his suicide.
Full name of the Russian-American author of Lolita and Pale Fire.
Tolstoy's epic novel of Russian aristocratic families during the Napoleonic Wars.
Chernyshevsky's radical novel envisioning a new socialist society, admired by Lenin.
Gogol's story of a lowly clerk's descent into delusions of Spanish royalty.
Chekhov's story on the moral compromise of a man who achieves his modest dream of a country estate.
Turgenev's story collection depicting serfs and rural gentry, seen as sympathetic to emancipation.
Demonic giant black cat in Bulgakov's The Master and Margarita.
Solzhenitsyn's semi-autobiographical novel set among patients in a Soviet oncology hospital.
Recurring demonic animal, notably Behemoth in The Master and Margarita.
Nabokov's surreal novel of a prisoner condemned for the vague crime of "gnostical turpitude."
Russian Silver Age poet who emigrated and later died by suicide after returning to the USSR.
Soviet literary journal that published Solzhenitsyn's One Day in the Life of Ivan Denisovich.
Full name of Dostoevsky's impoverished student who murders a pawnbroker in Crime and Punishment.
Underground self-published dissident literature circulated in the Soviet Union.
Appears as the urbane Woland who upends Stalinist Moscow in Bulgakov's The Master and Margarita.
Recurring 19th-century archetype of an alienated, purposeless aristocrat, as in Lermontov's Pechorin.
Gogol's satire of provincial officials mistaking a minor clerk for a feared government inspector.
Alternate English title of Gogol's satire of corrupt provincial bureaucrats.
Chekhov's drama of provincial sisters longing to return to Moscow.
Composer whose rivalry with Salieri is dramatized in Pushkin's short play Mozart and Salieri.
Gogol's absurdist story of a bureaucrat whose nose detaches and assumes a life of its own.
Pushkin's story of an officer's obsessive gamble for a card-game secret from an aged countess.
Chekhov's story of a doctor who grows indistinguishable from the asylum patients he treats.
Russian Symbolist poet; wrote "The Twelve" on the Russian Revolution.
Alternate spelling of the Soviet dissident Nobel laureate author of The Gulag Archipelago.
The youngest, spiritually devout Karamazov brother in Dostoevsky's novel.
Sholokhov's epic novel of Cossack life through revolution and civil war.
Composer dramatized as Mozart's jealous poisoner in Pushkin's short play Mozart and Salieri.
Frontier warrior communities depicted in Tolstoy's and Sholokhov's fiction.
Alternate title (Demons/The Possessed) of Dostoevsky's novel on a nihilist revolutionary conspiracy.
Condition afflicting Dostoevsky himself and his character Prince Myshkin in The Idiot.
Full name of the Russian existentialist-realist author of Crime and Punishment.
Soviet-Jewish writer; wrote Red Cavalry, on the Russian Civil War.
Chekhov's early play of a disillusioned landowner's despair and suicide.
Full name of the Russian Romantic author of A Hero of Our Time.
Russian capital and setting of Bulgakov's The Master and Margarita and the sisters' longing in Chekhov's Three Sisters.
Philosophy of rejecting all authority, dramatized through Turgenev's Bazarov in Fathers and Sons.
Russian writer known for the novella "Lady Macbeth of the Mtsensk District."
Dostoevsky's novella narrated by a bitter, isolated "underground man" railing against rationalism.
Dostoevsky's debut epistolary novel of an impoverished clerk's unrequited devotion.
Pushkin's fairy-tale narrative poem of a knight's quest to rescue his abducted bride.
Gogol's novella of a Cossack leader whose sons split between loyalty and love.
Bulgakov's tormented writer-protagonist in The Master and Margarita, author of the Pontius Pilate novel.
Dostoevsky's bitter, isolated narrator of Notes from Underground.
Russian Realist author of War and Peace and Anna Karenina.
Zamyatin's dystopian novel of a regimented future state, an influence on Orwell's 1984.
Gogol's folkloric tale of a young man's supernatural pact for treasure and love.
Chekhov's story of a wager over whether solitary confinement is worse than execution.
Chekhov's story of a scholar's visions of a ghostly monk, blurring genius and madness.
Full title of Pushkin's poem of a clerk driven mad by Peter the Great's statue during a flood.
Bunin's story of a wealthy tourist whose sudden death exposes life's vanity.
Gogol's horror story of a seminarian tormented by a witch and a monstrous folkloric spirit.
Dostoevsky's novella of a lonely dreamer's brief romance during St. Petersburg's white nights.
Recurring plot device settling honor disputes, notably in Eugene Onegin and Fathers and Sons.
Silver Age poetic movement favoring clarity and concreteness, led by Akhmatova and Mandelstam.

entries.answerline: Evenings on a Farm Near Dikanka

Contemporary Russian novelist known for transgressive satire of Soviet and post-Soviet society.

sections.name: Scandinavian Literature
sections.blurb: Norwegian, Swedish, Danish, and Icelandic authors, centered on Ibsen and Strindberg's revolutions in modern drama.

Strindberg's Naturalist tragedy of a countess's fatal liaison with her father's valet on Midsummer's Eve.
Ibsen's drama ending with Nora Helmer slamming the door on her marriage, a landmark feminist scene.

Home of Ibsen, Hamsun, and Undset.
Ibsen's drama on the destructive costs of forcing truth upon comforting illusions.
Ibsen's drama of a doctor ostracized for exposing his town's contaminated baths.

Ibsen's drama of an aging architect confronted by a young admirer, ending in a fatal fall.

Ibsen's drama of a former pastor and a woman haunted by his late wife's suicide.

Lagerkvist's novella narrated by a malevolent court dwarf during the Italian Renaissance.
Ibsen's final play of a sculptor confronting his former muse and lost artistic ideals.

Icelandic Nobel laureate; wrote Independent People.
Ibsen's fantastical verse drama following a boastful wanderer's journey of self-discovery.
Strindberg's chamber play exposing hidden sins behind a respectable household's facade.
Strindberg's fragmented, dreamlike drama following the daughter of a Hindu god through human suffering.
Laxness's novel of a stubborn Icelandic sheep farmer's struggle for self-sufficiency.
Ibsen's drama of a disgraced banker isolated after financial ruin and imprisonment.
Isak Dinesen's memoir of her years running a coffee farm in Kenya.

Strindberg's Naturalist tragedy of a cavalry captain driven mad by doubts over his daughter's paternity.
Isak Dinesen's story of a French refugee cook's lavish meal transforming an austere Danish village.

Ibsen's drama of a couple's guilt after their disabled son's drowning death.
Hereditary disease driving the tragedy of Ibsen's Ghosts.
Swedish Nobel laureate poet known for spare, image-driven verse.

sections.blurb: Czech, Polish, Hungarian, Balkan, and modern Greek writers, many shaped by twentieth-century totalitarianism and exile.

entries.topic: The Book of Laughter and Forgetting

Milosz's essay analyzing intellectuals' seduction by Stalinist ideology.

sections.name: British & Irish Literature

Irish Absurdist dramatist; wrote Waiting for Godot and Endgame.
Joyce's modernist novel tracing Leopold Bloom through a single Dublin day, echoing Homer's Odyssey.

Beckett's Absurdist play of Hamm and Clov trapped in a bare room after an apocalypse.

Beckett's Absurdist play of Vladimir and Estragon endlessly awaiting a figure who never arrives.

Shaw's play confronting prostitution and hypocritical Victorian morality.
Wilde's comedy of mistaken identities and double lives among the aristocracy.
Irish Nobel laureate poet of the Irish Literary Revival; wrote "The Second Coming."
